text: Yeah, so the title's actually kind of a pun or something.
So, yeah, so hi, I'm Paul Syverson from the Naval Research Lab.
If you don't know me, the thing I'm probably most known for is I invented onion routing
with a couple of my NRL colleagues forever ago, back in the 90s, and then a few years
later with a couple of non-NRL colleagues created a version of that we called Tor.
And this is the main thing I've been working on pretty much ever since.
What I've been working on mostly for the last several years is the question of there's a lot
of domain names that have associated onion addresses.
If you don't know what an onion address is, I don't have enough time to explain it.
So, for our purposes, you can just think of it as pretty much like a PLC did
because it's a cryptographically self-certifying address.
And domain names are generally human meaningful, and onion addresses are these cryptographically
things that are not, and turns out how to discover when things are associated and how to be sure
they're bound together has all kinds of problems that, has all kinds of problems, and which I
have spelled out in all of these papers that I'm not going to actually talk about, but that's
there if you want to go look for them, and then also, fortunately, solutions to these.
What are the goals of the work here is to sort of synthesize those things with,
at proto-identities, so handles and dids, with a couple of goals.
One is to improve and generalize blue sky verified accounts, to be more than they are now, explicit
about who the verifier is, and also to provide some context, to be decentralized, so it's not
limited just to the blue sky trusted verifiers, and also open, so that anybody can have a verified
identity, and in addition, we want to synthesize these together in a way that allows us to have self-certifying
meaningful identities that are consistently transparent, that are blocking resistant, this is one of the things
onion services provide in a way I won't explain, and robust to TLS certificate hijack, and also,
everything we've done is sort of fully backwards compatible, so that if you are running something
that doesn't know anything about our stuff, as long as it uses the standard protocols, nothing's
going to break.
You won't get the advantages, but nothing's going to break.
So this is joint work with the force of nature, who is Nick Jarakinas, who's right there.
And yes.
So suppose, as an example, I didn't know about smoke signal, and Nick says, oh, here's this
cool thing, smoke signal, you should check it out.
And let's assume that he and I have a trusted channel over signal, and we've validated that,
that I trust Nick about identifiers for domains, or maybe just for particular domains, we can
come back to that.
But what if smoke signal.events has a TLS hijack?
So just what I mean by that, well, if you go to smoke signal.events, you get the little,
the usual lock icon.
And if you click on that, you can see a certificate that says that an authority, in this case, let's
encrypt, says, oh, the key that's authenticating this connection is, in fact, associated with
the name smoke signal.
So that's great.
Only problem is, it's way too easy to obtain bogus TLS certificates.
It's not completely trivial, and a lot of people have done a lot of work to make it harder,
but I don't think there's a problem that's going away anytime soon.
It's just a big, bad problem.
So the answer, what if it gets hijacked, is somebody can basically set up their own version
of smoke signal, and get me to think whatever about upcoming events, or maybe reveal some
kind of sensitive information in the course of RSVPing, so in general, bad situation.
But what if instead of giving me just the link to smoke signal.events, the URL includes the
PLC did for smoke signal.events.
And again, we have the trusted channel that I'm getting things from, Nick from.
Now if I go and follow that URL, we have a Firefox extension that Nick created, that ultimately
I would like this to just be built into Firefox, but you need to crawl before you walk.
So this extension checks that there's a header that gets sent.
And it makes sure that the key that's on the header is in fact associated with this did,
and further that the did that's in the did document matches the did that was in the URL.
As opposed to if, for example, the did was not a good did, then you would fail the checks.
So great.
So this provides what I like to call dirt simple trust.
The idea is I need, all I need is the address and I'm kind of done.
That's enough.
I can't, I can't be hijacked or anything as long as we have this I discovered it in this
way.
Relatedly, it also offers what I would call authority independence.
So the idea is no authority, no CA can say, oh, no, here's the new key for, for smoke signal,
because they don't control the key that is associated with the did, I can't be tricked about that.
So because I learned of this URL from Nick, and because it passed the checks when I tried
to connect to it, I can trust that Nick told me that the handle and did are in fact bound
together, and further that, because I trust Nick, I can trust that they are bound together.
But we're not quite done, because in order to do this, we had to have this, you know, signal
exchange.
And Nick has been very tolerant of me, but I think if every time I wanted to have a secure
connection with anything, I had to have a signal communication with him, maybe that would
try even Nick's patients.
So we, therefore, have it automated so that we have a record that you get a signed header
that's signed by, say, njrakinis.me, as well as by smoke signal, that says that these are
bound together.
And further, and this is where I'm introducing contextual labels, it could have a label like,
for example, this is a Nick project, this is a project that Nick did.
So let's contrast this with the way Blue Sky currently handles verified accounts.
So you can, Blue Sky will directly verify somebody's account as belonging to someone, but they also
have a handful of trusted verifiers.
And that's fine, but it has some limitations.
For one thing, you have to be important in order to get a verified account.
Or you have to be affiliated with one of these, there's about 20 currently trusted verifiers.
Most of them are journalism organizations, and I mean, I think that's great, but the rest
of us are just SOL if we don't have, you know, one of these affiliations.
So what we have is it supports that kind of functionality, and I think should replace it.
So you can just do the same thing, that's what I've got here on the left, your left, whatever.
But it doesn't have to be centralized like that.
You could also have, say, like the town of Springfield is going to tell you what the local
cities are in Springfield.
So you might have a local news source in Springfield, and this is a good way you can
resist pink slime journalism because you won't have some, you know, somebody from far away
masquerading as being from Springfield.
Or it can also be even at the individual level.
So this scales both up and down.
So like if you're somebody, I just made this entity up, as far as I know, but you know,
you have somebody named Vegan Piper, maybe you trust them about where there are vegan restaurants
around.
But maybe you don't trust them about that.
But you do trust them about, you know, bagpipe supplies.
So you can choose which you want to trust them on.
